title: Agent Autonomy Levels
concept: From zero-autonomy copilots to fully autonomous loop execution, and the human oversight spectrum.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: an agent's 'observation space' is what it can see. Which of these could be an observation space?

options.text: A webpage's DOM tree, text logs, or JSON data

exercises.explain: An observation space is the format of information an agent takes in to understand the world, like text logs, DOM trees, visual frames, or structured JSON. That's how it 'sees' before it acts.

cards.title: How much leash?
cards.body: Some AI helpers wait for you to click every step. Others run for hours all by themselves. The difference is called autonomy.

cards.title: Autonomy Level
cards.body: Autonomy is how much an agent can decide and do on its own without a human. Low autonomy means it only suggests; high autonomy means it acts in a loop by itself.

cards.title: Learning to Drive
cards.body: A copilot is like a driving instructor who only gives tips while YOU steer. Full autonomy is a self-driving car that goes the whole trip alone. Most agents sit somewhere in between.

cards.title: Same Task, Different Levels
cards.body: Low: the AI suggests one email sentence, you type it. Medium: it drafts the whole email, you hit send. High: it writes AND sends replies all day without asking.

cards.title: Myth: More Autonomy Is Always Better
cards.body: Not true! High autonomy is powerful but risky if the agent makes a mistake unwatched. Good design matches the leash to how much you trust the task.

cards.title: Two Ends of the Spectrum
cards.body: On one side a human checks every move. On the other, the agent runs its own loop and only reports back.

left.text: Human approves each step; AI just suggests.

right.text: Agent plans, acts, and loops on its own.

options.text: It suggests ideas, but a human decides and does every step
It runs a full task loop for hours with no human
It sends emails without ever asking you
It shuts itself off and never helps

exercises.explain: A zero-autonomy copilot only assists. It offers suggestions, but the human stays in control and performs each action. It cannot act on its own.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Higher autonomy usually needs LESS human oversight per action.
exercises.explain: True. As autonomy rises, the agent handles more steps itself, so humans check fewer individual actions, often only reviewing the final result instead of every move.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about the oversight spectrum.
exercises.sentence: A fully autonomous agent runs its own loop without a human approving every step.
exercises.explain: A fully autonomous agent executes its task loop by itself, needing no human sign-off on each step, that's the high end of the autonomy spectrum.

exercises.prompt: Which description was written by an AI to sound like a helpful autonomy summary?

options.text: Autonomy levels range from copilots that only suggest, to agents that plan and act in a self-running loop, with human oversight decreasing as autonomy increases.
i think robots are kinda cool and stuff idk
Autonomy? Never heard of it, ask my cat.

exercises.explain: The AI-written option is the clear, complete, neutral summary that correctly describes the spectrum. The others are casual or dodge the question.

exercises.prompt: A company wants an agent to auto-refund customers. For a task where a wrong action costs real money, which autonomy setup is safest AND why?

options.text: Medium autonomy: the agent drafts the refund, but a human approves before it sends
Full autonomy with no human review, because it's faster
Zero help at all, humans do everything manually forever
Maximum autonomy because more autonomy is always better

exercises.explain: The right autonomy level depends on risk. For costly actions like refunds, keeping a human in the loop to approve each one catches mistakes before they cause harm. The spectrum runs from suggestion-only copilots to self-looping autonomous agents, and good design matches the amount of oversight to how much you can trust the task. That completes 'What is an AI Agent?', you now know how agents sense, decide, and act with the right amount of independence!
